student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Say Exactly What You Mean -- Writing the RFI
lesson.menu_desc: Seis unidades reprovaram no ensaio de EMC e quarenta foram construidas com o mesmo aterramento: oracoes relativas, relativas reduzidas e a virgula que decide o escopo do retrabalho -- 40 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 8 -- Say Exactly What You Mean
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 8 -- Say Exactly What You Mean

listenings.text: João, it is Ingrid. Your ten days are up tomorrow, and I would rather you heard this from me than read it in a report. I ran the emission scan on four of the units that passed, as I said I would. Two of them are emitting just under the limit. They passed, but they passed by almost nothing, and a unit that passes by almost nothing is a unit that will fail on a bad day. So the design is marginal across the whole batch, and the six that failed were simply the ones in the worst position. You were right, and I was arguing a position rather than a finding. The grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, was verified on the bench and it was never verified in the installed condition. That is our gap, not yours, and it will be re-verified for all forty at our cost. You will have the certification standard and the full test conditions tomorrow morning, in writing, as you asked. Now the part I have to ask, and you are free to say no. Please keep the trial run on the ferrite filters, so that the line is not stopped while we do this properly. And one more thing. The sentence you wrote in that RFI is the reason I went back and looked. I could not read it two ways, so I had to go and find out which way it was.

Before you submit that RFI, three things, because engineers write these badly more often than anybody else. First, the comma. In a technical document the comma is not punctuation, it is scope, and scope is money. Write the units that failed the test, with no commas, and you have restricted it: those units, six of them, and nobody else. Write the units, comma, which failed the test, comma, and you have just told a German engineering team that all forty of their units failed, which is not true, and which is the first thing their lawyer will point out to you. It is the cheapest mistake in the language to make and the most expensive one to argue about afterwards. Second, and this is the one that wastes a month. Never put the word problem in a request for information. A problem is not a measurement. A problem invites an opinion, and you will get six opinions and no answer. State what was found, against which criterion, on which date, and then ask a question that can only be answered one way. A finding invites a response. Third, and this is the one that costs thirty years. Accept a workaround only with the date it dies. Write the filters in, if you must, but write in the day they come out, and write in what has to be in place before they come out. A temporary fix with no end date is not temporary. It quietly becomes the design, it goes into the as-built drawings, and the operator pays to maintain it long after everybody who agreed to it has left the project.

patterns.title: Four clauses that carry a technical query
cards: No commas. The clause narrows the noun down. Take it away and you no longer know what you are talking about. This is the clause that sets the scope of the work. "The six interlocking units that failed the EMC test must be reworked."
You add a fact about ALL of them

Two commas. The clause is extra information, true of every one of them. Take it away and the sentence still stands. Never use that here -- only which. "The grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, must be re-verified."
Drop which was / that were and keep the past participle. This is how every specification and every standard on your shelf is written. "The report issued on 14 July does not cover the grounding."
The preposition goes BEFORE which -- and after a preposition it can never be that. The register of the RFI itself. "Please confirm the standard to which the design was certified."

patterns.text: The comma is not punctuation in a technical document. It is scope, and scope is money. Six units, or forty: the words are identical and only the comma decides.

reading.rtitle: Response to RFI-114 -- EMC / Grounding, Interlocking Units (supplier, extract)
paras: We acknowledge receipt of RFI-114, submitted on 16 July, concerning the electromagnetic compatibility of the interlocking units delivered for Line 5. The interlocking units which were found to exceed the emission limit during the test performed on 14 July will be reworked at our cost, and the units concerned will be returned to site within six weeks. The test report issued by our laboratory is attached for your records.

With regard to the grounding configuration, which is common to the whole batch, we do not consider that the evidence supports a design non-compliance. Thirty-four of the forty units delivered were tested against the same criteria and passed. The six units that did not pass are the six installed closest to the traction return cable, and the interference is therefore attributable to the conditions under which they were installed, rather than to the equipment as designed. Any re-verification of the configuration itself would fall outside the scope of the present contract, and a separate proposal would be required.

In the interim, our engineering team has devised a workaround which can be implemented at short notice: ferrite filters, fitted to the affected units, which bring the emissions within the limit. We are able to confirm that the units so modified will meet the acceptance criteria applicable to the trial run. We would ask you to confirm your agreement by return, so that the commissioning programme is not delayed further.
reading.source: Supplier Engineering Department -- Response to RFI-114, extract (fictional, written for João's package)

reading.text: Notice the collocations: submit an RFI, exceed the emission limit, perform a test, issue a report, devise a workaround, fall outside the scope, meet the acceptance criteria, verify compliance.

gist.prompt: What is this letter actually doing?
choices: It accepts the finding and confirms that all forty units will be re-verified at the supplier's cost.
It accepts the six units, and uses a restrictive clause to keep the grounding configuration -- which affects all forty -- outside the scope of the contract, offering a workaround instead of a fix.
It rejects the finding entirely and refuses to rework any of the units.
It asks the client to confirm the standard to which the design was certified before any rework begins.

items: The supplier agrees to rework the six units that failed, at its own cost.
t
"The interlocking units which were found to exceed the emission limit... will be reworked at our cost." The clause has no commas: it restricts the promise to those units only.
The supplier accepts that the grounding configuration must be re-verified.
f

The opposite. "Any re-verification of the configuration itself would fall outside the scope of the present contract, and a separate proposal would be required." That is an RFP -- they are asking to be paid for it.
The supplier argues that the six failures were caused by where the units were installed.
t

"...the interference is therefore attributable to the conditions under which they were installed, rather than to the equipment as designed." The preposition + which is doing the work: it points at the installation, not the design.
The ferrite filters are offered as a permanent fix that meets the handover criteria.
f

"...the units so modified will meet the acceptance criteria applicable to the trial run." The trial run -- not the handover. The reduced relative narrows the promise, and it is easy to miss on a fast read.
The supplier states that all forty units were tested in the installed condition.
f

It never says that. It says thirty-four "were tested against the same criteria and passed" -- and it is silent about the condition in which the configuration itself was verified. What a document does not say is where the money hides.

items.situation: "So we are agreed: six units." Accept the six -- and do not let the design question close with it.
tips: We agree on the six. The six interlocking units that failed the EMC test on 14 July are to be reworked at your cost.
What we do not agree on is why. Those six were built to the grounding configuration, which is common to all forty.

items.situation: "Thirty-four units passed. If the design were the cause, all forty would have failed." Refuse the inference without calling her wrong.
tips: That is a reasonable hypothesis, and it may well be right. But it is a hypothesis, not a finding.
Thirty-four units passing a test does not demonstrate a design. It demonstrates thirty-four units, on one day, in one position.

items.situation: "Re-verification would fall outside the contract. We would need to issue a proposal." Say why that is not an RFP.
tips: I am afraid that is not new scope. The configuration was never verified in the installed condition, and the contract requires compliance as installed.
This is a request for information, not a request for proposal. I am asking you to explain what you already owe.

items.situation: "The ferrite filters solve it. One week and the line runs." Take the workaround without buying it.
tips: I will take the filters for the trial run. I will not take them for the handover.
A workaround does not meet the acceptance criteria that apply at handover, and those criteria are in clause 7.4.

items.situation: "What exactly do you want in writing?" Two formal questions, preposition first.
tips: Please confirm the standard to which the design was certified.
And spell out the conditions under which the EMC test was performed -- on the bench, or in the installed condition.

items.situation: "Can we close this by Friday?" Say what closes it, and what does not.
tips: What closes it is the standard, the test conditions, and a scan on the units that passed. Not an agreement to disagree.
Until we pin down the source of the interference, any fix we agree today is a guess, and we would both be signing it.

bank.label: Restricting the scope (no commas)
items: The six interlocking units that failed the EMC test must be reworked at the supplier's cost.
The workaround that your team devised does not meet the acceptance criteria that apply at handover.
The RFI that we submitted on 16 July has not been answered in full.

bank.label: Adding the fact about all of them (two commas)
items: The grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, must be re-verified.
The emission limit, which is defined in clause 7.4, applies at handover and not only at the trial run.
Ingrid Bauer, who leads the systems team on their side, has committed to the test conditions in writing.

bank.label: Compressing like a technical document
items: The report issued on 14 July does not cover the grounding configuration.
The units radiating above the limit are the six installed closest to the traction return cable.
The cable runs installed before March have not been re-tested.

bank.label: Asking formally (preposition first)
items: Please confirm the standard to which the design was certified.
Please spell out the conditions under which the EMC test was performed.
Please identify the clause on which your interpretation of the scope is based.
